answer:
**Bang-e-Dara** summons a dormant Muslim *ummah* (community) already present but spiritually asleep, as in Allama Iqbal's direct address to the "Muslim youth" in *Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*:  

> Have you ever,  
> O Muslim youth, paused for solemn reflection?[^1]  

Iqbal presumes his audience already identifies as Muslim, chiding their complacency against their ancestors' legacy ("You can have no true kinship with your forefathers, for you are all talk, they were all action").[^2]

**Andreas**, by contrast, depicts St. Andrew preaching amid cannibalistic Mermedonians—a heathen populace with no Christian community whatsoever. The poem's most summoning line is God's command to Andrew, which launches the mission to conjure one from nothing:  

> “You must go now, and chart a journey,  
> fare over the flood-path to a far country,  
> the land of the man-eaters, Mermedonia.[^3]  

Andrew's embedded sermons (e.g., rebuking devils, proclaiming monotheism) presume *zero* shared faith, converting foes via miracles.[^4] Cynewulf's frame-narrative also hails an *existing* Christian audience ("We have heard of heroes in ages past"),[^5] but the core "sermon" presumes to birth belief ex nihilo.

**Andreas** is more presumptuous: *Bang-e-Dara* rings a bell over sleepers who already claim the faith; *Andreas* commands a missionary to forge hearers from man-eaters.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.13 [*Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.13 [*Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).

[^3]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch.1 (/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-4d1e78).

[^4]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch.1 (/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca8f6e).

[^5]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch.1 (/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-868413).
